skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: This skill is a legitimate mobile design and engineering doctrine. All 758 static scanner detections are false positives: backtick characters in markdown documentation, mentions of security terms (Keychain, SAM) in educational context about secure coding practices, and references to cryptographic algorithms in warnings against weak crypto. The included mobile_audit.py is a genuine React Native/Flutter code analysis tool for mobile-specific performance and security issues.

content.user_title: Build Mobile Apps with Design Best Practices
content.value_statement: Mobile apps fail when developers apply desktop thinking. This skill teaches mobile-first design principles, touch psychology, performance optimization, and platform-specific conventions for iOS and Android.
actual_capabilities: Apply mobile-first design principles to prevent desktop-thinking in app development
Evaluate feature feasibility using the Mobile Feasibility and Risk Index (MFRI)
Implement platform-appropriate navigation patterns for iOS and Android
Optimize React Native and Flutter performance with memoization and FlatList patterns
Design accessible touch targets meeting iOS (44pt) and Android (48dp) guidelines
Apply Fitts Law and thumb zone principles for touch-friendly interfaces
limitations: Does not write application code - provides design guidance and decision frameworks
Does not cover backend API implementation details
Does not include visual design assets or component libraries
Requires manual application of principles to specific project contexts

use_cases.title: Mobile App Architecture Decisions
use_cases.description: Use the framework decision tree and MFRI to evaluate whether to build with React Native, Flutter, or native frameworks based on project requirements.

use_cases.title: Performance Optimization Review
use_cases.description: Apply the mobile performance doctrine to audit existing React Native or Flutter code for common performance sins like ScrollView abuse, missing memoization, and console.log in production.

use_cases.title: Cross-Platform Design Validation
use_cases.description: Use the platform unification matrix to decide which elements should be unified across iOS and Android versus platform-specific implementations.
use_cases.target_user: UI/UX designers working on cross-platform apps

prompt_templates.title: New Feature Feasibility Check
prompt_templates.prompt: Using the MFRI framework from the mobile-design skill, evaluate this feature request for a [platform] app: [describe feature]. What is the feasibility score and what considerations must be addressed?
prompt_templates.scenario: Evaluating a new mobile feature before implementation

prompt_templates.title: Code Review for Performance
prompt_templates.prompt:
Review this React Native/Flutter code snippet for mobile performance issues. Check for: ScrollView vs FlatList, React.memo usage, useCallback for renderItem, keyExtractor with stable IDs, and console.log statements that should be removed:

```
[paste code here]
```
prompt_templates.scenario: Auditing mobile code for performance anti-patterns

prompt_templates.title: Platform-Specific UI Decision
prompt_templates.prompt: For a cross-platform mobile app, determine which UI elements should DIVERGE between iOS and Android based on the mobile-design skill platform matrix. Example element: [navigation pattern]. Provide recommendations with rationale.
prompt_templates.scenario: Deciding on platform-specific vs unified UI approaches

prompt_templates.title: Touch Accessibility Audit
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this mobile screen for touch accessibility: Check touch target sizes (minimum 44pt iOS, 48dp Android), thumb zone placement for primary CTAs, gesture alternatives for swipe actions, and haptic feedback on important actions.
prompt_templates.scenario: Evaluating mobile screen for touch accessibility compliance

output_examples.input: How should I implement navigation in a React Native app for both iOS and Android?
output_examples.output: Based on mobile-design principles: 1) Use React Navigation with bottom tabs (max 5 items), 2) Implement lazy: false to preserve tab state, 3) Add BackHandler listener for Android hardware back button, 4) Use iOS-safe area handling, 5) Consider platform-specific navigation patterns - iOS uses edge swipe while Android uses system back.

output_examples.input: What are the critical performance issues to avoid in React Native lists?
output_examples.output: Critical issues: 1) NEVER use ScrollView with .map() for long lists - use FlatList, 2) Always wrap list items in React.memo, 3) Use useCallback for renderItem function, 4) NEVER use index as key - use stable IDs, 5) Use useNativeDriver: true for animations, 6) Remove all console.log statements before production.

best_practices: Always assess feature feasibility with MFRI before implementation - scores below 3 require redesign
Read platform-specific files (platform-ios.md, platform-android.md) before designing any mobile UI
Apply the Mandatory Mobile Checkpoint before writing any code - document platform, framework, and design principles
anti_patterns: Applying desktop web patterns to mobile (scrollable divs, hover states, large typography)
Ignoring platform conventions - iOS and Android have different navigation, gestures, and component patterns
Skipping offline consideration - mobile users frequently lose connectivity and expect graceful degradation

faq.question: What is the MFRI score threshold for proceeding with a feature?
faq.answer: MFRI scores of 6-10 are safe to proceed normally. Scores of 3-5 require additional performance and UX validation. Scores of 0-2 require simplifying the interactions or architecture. Scores below 0 mean the feature should be redesigned before implementation.

faq.question: When should I choose React Native over Flutter?
faq.answer: Choose React Native when: you need OTA updates without app store review, your team has existing React experience, or you want to share code with a web application. Choose Flutter when you need pixel-perfect custom UI across platforms or maximum performance for graphics-intensive apps.

faq.question: What is the minimum touch target size for mobile?
faq.answer: iOS requires minimum 44pt touch targets, Android requires 48dp, and WCAG accessibility standard requires 44px. Always aim for at least 48px to satisfy all platforms and accessibility requirements.

faq.question: How do I handle offline functionality in mobile apps?
faq.answer: Mobile backend must be designed for unreliable networks. Use offline-first design with sync queues, implement NetInfo for connection status detection, cache API responses appropriately, and design for interrupted sessions with token-based authentication and refresh tokens.

faq.question: Why should I avoid AsyncStorage for sensitive data?
faq.answer: AsyncStorage stores data in plain text and can be easily accessed on jailbroken devices or through app data extraction. For sensitive data like auth tokens, use platform-specific secure storage: iOS Keychain (SecureStore) or Android EncryptedSharedPreferences.

faq.question: What makes mobile testing different from web testing?
faq.answer: Mobile requires testing on real devices (not just simulators), E2E testing with Detox or Maestro, accessibility testing with screen readers, and performance profiling on low-end devices. The mobile audit script included in this skill checks for 50+ mobile-specific issues.
